title: Airflow Requirement Guide
summary: Understand how heat load, allowed temperature rise, and air-density correction affect required cooling airflow.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates the airflow needed to remove a known heat load while staying within an allowed temperature rise.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid before committing to equipment, racks, fans, or cooling assumptions.
Use it to document the load, margin, and environmental assumptions behind the design.
Use it to catch thermal pressure before it becomes a field reliability problem.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when sizing cabinet fans, room ventilation, rack airflow, closet exhaust, or any cooling path where heat must be carried away by moving air.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-room or cabinet validation.
Explaining cooling and heat-removal tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical thermal planning terms.

items.label: Heat Load (Watts)
items.description: The amount of electrical load that becomes heat and must be removed from the space or enclosure.
items.whyItMatters: Higher heat load requires more airflow for the same allowed temperature rise.
items.commonMistake: Using only one device rating when the fan or room must cool the full combined load.

links.label: Estimate heat load from device watts
links.note: Use this when the heat load has not been totaled yet.

items.label: Allowed ΔT (°F)
items.description: The maximum temperature rise allowed between intake/supply air and exhaust/return air.
items.whyItMatters: A lower allowed rise requires more airflow; a higher allowed rise may increase equipment inlet or exhaust temperature risk.
items.commonMistake: Picking an allowed rise that looks convenient instead of one that matches equipment and room limits.

links.label: Check what airflow causes as ambient rise
links.note: Use this when airflow is known and you need to estimate rise instead.

items.label: Air Density Factor
items.description: A correction factor for how effectively air carries heat under the assumed site conditions.
items.whyItMatters: Less dense or less effective air needs more volume flow to remove the same heat.
items.commonMistake: Leaving the factor at ideal conditions for hot rooms, restricted paths, or higher elevations.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means required airflow is reasonable for the entered heat load and temperature-rise target. Watch or Risk means airflow demand may be difficult to deliver through the actual room, rack, cabinet, filter, or fan path.
bullets: Healthy: the entered assumptions appear manageable with reasonable thermal margin.
Watch: the design may still work, but airflow, load, restriction, or cooling margin should be reviewed.
Risk: reduce heat load, improve airflow/cooling, add margin, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Thermal planning mistakes usually happen when heat load, airflow, and cooling capacity are sized separately instead of as one operating envelope.
bullets: Treating nameplate power, measured load, airflow, and cooling capacity as interchangeable values.
Ignoring restriction, recirculation, altitude, inlet temperature, and future equipment growth.
Using ideal fan or cooling ratings as if they are guaranteed sustained field performance.
Forgetting that nearly all electrical load eventually becomes heat that must be removed.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, mechanical design review, HVAC validation, commissioning data, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Vendor specifications and site measurements still matter.
Actual airflow depends on restriction, path geometry, filter condition, fan curves, and cabinet leakage.
Cooling designs should be verified against the actual equipment, rack layout, and room conditions.
A design with margin is usually easier to support during summer conditions, maintenance, and expansion.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, site-specific thermal testing, code compliance review, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual equipment, room, rack, cabinet, and cooling system being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations, operating limits, and maintenance requirements.
Document assumptions before implementation so thermal decisions can be reviewed later.
